question_text: The Indus Waters Treaty (1960) between India and Pakistan is often cited as one of the most successful bilateral treaties in South Asia. What does this treaty allocate and why is it considered remarkable?
explanation: The Indus Waters Treaty was signed on September 19, 1960, by PM Jawaharlal Nehru and Pakistani President Ayub Khan, brokered by the World Bank. It divided the six rivers of the Indus system between the two countries: India received exclusive use of the three eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) while Pakistan received the three larger western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab). The treaty is remarkable because: it has survived three India-Pakistan wars (1965, 1971, 1999); it established a Permanent Indus Commission with annual meetings even during periods of conflict; and it has functioned continuously for over 60 years despite the deep hostility between the two countries. It is considered a model of technical cooperation transcending political conflict.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Divides six Indus rivers between India and Pakistan — India gets eastern rivers, Pakistan western — has survived three wars, functioning for 60+ years
Divides the waters of all rivers flowing from India to Pakistan equally — has been renegotiated three times after each war
Gives India complete control over all Himalayan rivers with Pakistan receiving annual financial compensation
Was terminated after the 1971 war and replaced by the Shimla Agreement's water provisions

elimination_hints.hint: The treaty does not divide rivers equally — it gave India the three smaller eastern rivers and Pakistan the three larger western rivers; it has not been renegotiated but maintained continuously.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume equal division when the Indus Treaty specifically allocated different rivers to each country based on irrigation dependencies.

elimination_hints.hint: Pakistan received the three largest Indus system rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) — the opposite of India having complete control; the treaty specifically protects Pakistan's water security.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume the stronger party (India) got all the rivers when the treaty specifically gave the larger rivers to Pakistan which is more agriculturally dependent on Indus water.

elimination_hints.hint: The Indus Waters Treaty was not terminated — it has functioned continuously since 1960 through all three wars; the Shimla Agreement (1972) dealt with Kashmir, not water.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume the 1971 war and Shimla Agreement replaced the Indus Treaty when it has been one of the few India-Pakistan agreements to survive intact.

question_text: The Siachen Glacier dispute is a unique India-Pakistan conflict involving the world's highest battlefield. What is the dispute about and why does India control Siachen?
explanation: The Siachen Glacier, located in the Karakoram range at altitudes above 6,000 metres, became a military conflict zone in 1984 when India launched Operation Meghdoot (April 13, 1984) and occupied the glacier before Pakistan could. The dispute arose because the 1972 Shimla Agreement's Line of Control demarcation ended at map coordinate NJ9842 — beyond which both sides claimed the high-altitude glacier. India moved first, airlifting troops to occupy the key passes. Pakistan has challenged Indian occupation. The conflict has cost both sides thousands of soldiers — primarily to the harsh conditions rather than combat — making it the world's highest and arguably costliest territorial dispute relative to the strategic value of frozen, uninhabited terrain.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: India controls Siachen because the Shimla Agreement explicitly awarded the glacier to India
Pakistan voluntarily withdrew from Siachen in 1984 as part of a secret diplomatic deal
India preemptively occupied Siachen in 1984 (Operation Meghdoot) before Pakistan could, exploiting the Shimla Agreement's ambiguity beyond NJ9842
A UN arbitration tribunal awarded Siachen to India based on its greater military investment in the region

elimination_hints.hint: The Shimla Agreement did not mention Siachen — its LoC demarcation ended at NJ9842 before reaching the glacier, which is precisely why the dispute exists.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume the comprehensive Shimla Agreement would have addressed all territorial questions when its LoC explicitly ended before the Siachen area.

elimination_hints.hint: Pakistan did not voluntarily withdraw — India moved first in Operation Meghdoot to occupy key passes; Pakistan has military positions on the glacier and has contested India's occupation ever since.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume a diplomatic explanation for India's Siachen presence when it was a preemptive military occupation.

elimination_hints.hint: No UN tribunal awarded Siachen to India — the UN has no jurisdiction in this bilateral dispute which India refuses to internationalise; India's occupation rests on the military fact of its 1984 operation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine international legal resolution for a dispute that India specifically maintains as a bilateral military fact-on-the-ground.

question_text: Pakistan's foreign policy has historically relied on which major external power as a strategic counterweight to India, and what specific military and economic assistance has this relationship provided?
explanation: Pakistan has maintained a strategic partnership with the United States since the 1950s, joining US-led alliances SEATO (1954) and CENTO (1955) during the Cold War. The US-Pakistan relationship has provided: military equipment (F-16 fighter jets, Harpoon missiles, artillery), economic aid (billions in development and military assistance), and intelligence cooperation (particularly during the Afghan jihad 1979-89 and post-9/11). Pakistan has also developed a deep strategic partnership with China since the 1960s — when India-China and India-Pakistan interests converged. China has provided nuclear technology (according to international reports), missile systems, the JF-17 Thunder fighter aircraft, and the China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC). India sees both the US-Pakistan and China-Pakistan relationships as strategic challenges, particularly the China-Pakistan axis.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Russia — which has provided Pakistan with nuclear technology, MiG aircraft, and anti-India security guarantees since 1947
USA and China — the USA through Cold War alliance membership and military aid; China through nuclear/missile technology, JF-17, and CPEC infrastructure
Iran — which shares Pakistan's Islamic identity and has provided critical oil and military technology
Saudi Arabia — which has provided fighter jets, nuclear weapons, and commands Pakistan's military strategy

elimination_hints.hint: Russia has been traditionally aligned with India, not Pakistan — the Indo-Soviet Treaty (1971) was used during the Bangladesh War; Pakistan has not been a Russian ally.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the Cold War alignments — the USSR/Russia was India's partner while the USA was Pakistan's Cold War ally.

elimination_hints.hint: Iran and Pakistan have complex relations often marked by sectarian tension (Iran is Shia-majority, Pakistani militant groups have targeted Shia) — Iran has not been a major military patron of Pakistan.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume shared Islamic identity creates a Pakistan-Iran strategic alliance when sectarian differences and competing regional interests limit that relationship.

elimination_hints.hint: Saudi Arabia provides financial support and has some influence over Pakistani religious politics, but Saudi Arabia has no fighter jets or nuclear weapons to provide; it does not command Pakistan's military.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overstate Saudi Arabia's military role in Pakistan, confusing financial and religious influence with military hardware provision and strategic command.

question_text: The Cross-LoC Bus Service from Srinagar to Muzaffarabad, inaugurated in April 2005, was a significant confidence-building measure (CBM) in India-Pakistan relations. What was the significance of this initiative?
explanation: The Srinagar-Muzaffarabad bus service, inaugurated on April 7, 2005, was the first bus link across the Line of Control in 57 years — since partition. Its significance: (1) it reconnected divided Kashmiri families separated by the LoC since 1947; (2) it was a symbolic acknowledgement that the LoC need not be an insurmountable barrier despite the unresolved sovereignty dispute; (3) it operated as a CBM — each step of normalisation aimed to build trust incrementally; (4) it demonstrated that both sides could implement practical cooperation without resolving the fundamental political dispute. The bus service operated periodically, was suspended during tensions, and represented the kind of incremental people-to-people contact that advocates of normalisation argue can build long-term peace.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: It resolved the Kashmir dispute by creating a permanent open border between India and Pakistan
It was Pakistan's formal recognition that the LoC would become the permanent international boundary
It allowed Pakistan to deploy intelligence agents into India to monitor Indian military positions
It reconnected divided Kashmiri families separated since 1947 and symbolised that practical cooperation could occur despite unresolved sovereignty disputes

elimination_hints.hint: The bus service explicitly did not resolve the Kashmir dispute or create an open border — it was a specific humanitarian CBM that both sides carefully designed to avoid prejudicing the sovereignty question.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume practical connectivity measures imply political resolution when both governments specifically stated the bus service did not affect the legal status of the LoC.

elimination_hints.hint: Pakistan explicitly stated the bus service did not constitute recognition of the LoC as an international border — maintaining its position on Kashmir's disputed status.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume Pakistan accepting a cross-LoC bus service meant accepting the LoC as a permanent boundary, when Pakistan carefully avoided any such implication.

elimination_hints.hint: The bus service was a civilian humanitarian initiative — it did not serve as an intelligence platform; both sides vetted passengers extensively.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume practical India-Pakistan contacts are inevitably intelligence operations rather than genuine civilian humanitarian measures.

question_text: The concept of 'nuclear deterrence' in South Asia has been described as both stabilising and destabilising. How does India's nuclear doctrine differ from Pakistan's nuclear doctrine in terms of declared policy?
explanation: India's nuclear doctrine (officially articulated in 2003) rests on three pillars: (1) No First Use (NFU) — India will not use nuclear weapons first; (2) Credible Minimum Deterrence — India maintains only the minimum force necessary for deterrence; and (3) Massive Retaliation — if India suffers a nuclear attack, retaliation will be 'massive and designed to inflict unacceptable damage.' India's NFU has a caveat about responding to chemical/biological weapons attacks. Pakistan explicitly rejects NFU — it maintains 'full spectrum deterrence' including the right to use nuclear weapons first if facing catastrophic conventional defeat. Pakistan's doctrine is designed to deter India's Cold Start doctrine (rapid conventional military operations). The asymmetry — India NFU, Pakistan first-use option — creates the stability-instability paradox.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Both India and Pakistan have adopted No First Use doctrines — the difference is only in what constitutes 'first use'
India maintains a first-use option while Pakistan has adopted No First Use to signal peaceful intentions
India has adopted No First Use with massive retaliation; Pakistan rejects NFU and maintains a first-use option to deter conventional Indian military advances
Both India and Pakistan have rejected No First Use — both maintain first-use options to maximise deterrence

elimination_hints.hint: Pakistan has explicitly rejected NFU — this is a fundamental and publicly stated difference between the two doctrines, not just a definitional variation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students smooth over the sharp doctrinal difference by assuming both have NFU with definitional variations.

elimination_hints.hint: India adopted NFU, Pakistan rejected it — this is the reverse of B's claim.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reverse the doctrinal positions of India and Pakistan on No First Use.

elimination_hints.hint: India has formally and publicly adopted NFU as a stated policy — claiming India has rejected NFU contradicts India's official nuclear doctrine.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume all nuclear states maintain first-use options when India has specifically and formally adopted NFU.

question_text: Pakistan's Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) has been described as a 'state within a state'. What specific role has the ISI played in India-Pakistan relations that India has consistently cited as a major obstacle to normalisation?
explanation: The ISI (Pakistan's premier intelligence agency) has been accused by India of: (1) directing, funding, and equipping militant organisations like Lashkar-e-Taiba (responsible for Mumbai 2008), Jaish-e-Mohammed (Pulwama 2019, Parliament 2001), and Hizbul Mujahideen operating in Kashmir; (2) providing sanctuary to terrorist leaders — Hafiz Saeed (LeT founder) lived openly in Lahore despite a $10 million US bounty; (3) using jihadi proxies as an instrument of foreign policy to impose costs on India below the nuclear threshold; and (4) supporting the Taliban in Afghanistan, which then provided bases for anti-India operations. India's consistent position has been that meaningful dialogue requires Pakistan to 'dismantle the terrorist infrastructure' — a condition the Pakistani civilian government claims it cannot enforce against the ISI.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Directing, funding, and equipping militant organisations that conduct attacks in India — particularly in Kashmir — while civilian governments claim inability to control the ISI
Conducting economic espionage against Indian companies to steal industrial secrets for Pakistani businesses
Monitoring Pakistani diplomatic communications for India's benefit as part of a secret bilateral intelligence sharing arrangement
Purely defensive — gathering intelligence about Indian military movements along the LoC without any offensive operations against India

elimination_hints.hint: Economic espionage may occur but is not the ISI's primary role cited by India as an obstacle to normalisation — India's specific complaints are about militant organisations and terrorism, not industrial espionage.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students deflect from the terrorism-focused allegations to imagine economic espionage as the primary concern.

elimination_hints.hint: No secret bilateral ISI-RAW intelligence sharing arrangement exists — the two agencies are adversarial; India's concerns about the ISI are about its offensive militant support operations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine cooperative intelligence relations when India-Pakistan intelligence relations are adversarial.

elimination_hints.hint: The ISI's offensive operations supporting militant groups in Kashmir and India are extensively documented — India cites specific attacks (Parliament 2001, Mumbai 2008, Pulwama 2019) as ISI-directed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students accept Pakistan's official narrative that the ISI operates purely defensively when India has documented specific ISI-linked terrorist attacks.

question_text: India's 'Cold Start' military doctrine, reportedly developed after the 2001-02 military standoff, was designed to address which specific strategic problem in the India-Pakistan context?
explanation: Cold Start doctrine was reportedly developed by the Indian Army after the 2001-02 standoff (Operation Parakram), when India spent six months mobilising 500,000 troops to the Pakistan border — a mobilisation so slow that by the time forces were ready, international pressure had forced de-escalation. Cold Start envisions rapid integrated battle groups launching limited offensive operations into Pakistan within 72-96 hours of an attack, before: international diplomacy can intervene to stop India's response; the USA can pressure India to stand down; and critically, before Pakistan has time to move to nuclear use. The doctrine is controversial because: Pakistan responded by developing tactical nuclear weapons (Nasr/Hatf-IX missiles) to deter conventional Indian breakthroughs — potentially lowering the nuclear threshold dangerously.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Developing India's nuclear second-strike capability through submarine-based missiles
Creating a defensive posture to protect India from Pakistani nuclear first strikes
Establishing India's ability to intervene militarily in Afghanistan before Pakistan
Enabling rapid conventional military operations that achieve objectives before international pressure or Pakistani nuclear escalation can intervene

elimination_hints.hint: India's submarine-based nuclear capability is developed separately through the SSBN programme — Cold Start is a conventional army doctrine, not a nuclear capability programme.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse India's nuclear deterrence development (submarines) with Cold Start which is a conventional rapid-deployment army doctrine.

elimination_hints.hint: Cold Start is an offensive rapid strike doctrine, not a defensive shield against nuclear attacks — it is India's conventional offensive option designed for punitive retaliation against Pakistan.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume Cold Start is defensive when it is explicitly an offensive doctrine for rapid conventional military penetration into Pakistani territory.

elimination_hints.hint: Cold Start is entirely Pakistan-oriented — it has nothing to do with Afghanistan; the 'cold' refers to rapid mobilisation from cold standing start, not geography.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Cold Start's Pakistani focus with India's Afghan policy, perhaps because Afghanistan also borders Pakistan.

question_text: India and Pakistan's trade relationship has been described as vastly underperforming its potential. What are the primary reasons that bilateral trade remains far below what economic complementarity would suggest?
explanation: India-Pakistan bilateral trade was only approximately $2-3 billion annually (pre-2019) compared to the $37 billion that the Asian Development Bank estimated as potential. Reasons for underperformance: (1) Pakistan maintained a 'Positive List' allowing only specific Indian goods rather than open trade; (2) India withdrew Most Favoured Nation (MFN) status from Pakistan after Pulwama (2019); (3) the two countries use third countries (UAE, Singapore) for indirect trade — adding costs; (4) Pakistan's army fears that open trade would create economic dependencies making military confrontation less viable; (5) banking restrictions prevented payment mechanisms; (6) visa restrictions limited businesspeople's travel; and (7) political hostility periodically caused suspension of trade links. Both countries lose economically from this underperformance.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Both economies produce identical goods making trade economically pointless — there is no economic complementarity between India and Pakistan
Political hostility, restricted trade lists, MFN withdrawal, army's opposition to economic dependency on India, and banking/visa restrictions all prevent trade from reaching its potential
WTO rules prevent India and Pakistan from trading with each other until their territorial dispute is resolved
China has blocked India-Pakistan trade to prevent any normalisation that might reduce its influence over Pakistan

elimination_hints.hint: India and Pakistan have significant economic complementarity — India's pharmaceuticals, machinery, and textiles complement Pakistan's agricultural goods and textiles; the ADB estimated $37 billion potential.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume two South Asian countries with similar colonial economic histories have identical products and no complementarity.

elimination_hints.hint: WTO rules do not prevent bilateral trade between countries with territorial disputes — India trades with China despite massive border disputes; WTO rules actually encourage open trade regardless of political disputes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine WTO has a political conditionality linking trade rights to territorial dispute resolution when WTO is specifically a non-political trade rules body.

elimination_hints.hint: China has no mechanism to block India-Pakistan trade and would have no interest in preventing normalisation that might reduce regional tension; India-Pakistan trade restrictions are domestically generated.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students attribute India-Pakistan trade failures to Chinese manipulation when the restrictions are self-imposed by both governments for their own political and security reasons.

question_text: The Balakot airstrike of February 2019 was historically significant in India-Pakistan relations. What happened and why was it unprecedented?
explanation: On February 26, 2019, the Indian Air Force conducted airstrikes on what it claimed was a Jaish-e-Mohammed training camp in Balakot, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan — the first Indian Air Force strike inside undisputed Pakistani territory since the 1971 war. The strike came in response to the Pulwama attack (February 14, 2019) in which a JeM suicide bomber killed 40 Indian CRPF personnel in Kashmir. Pakistan responded with its own airstrikes the next day (February 27), and one IAF aircraft (MiG-21 Bison flown by Wing Commander Abhinandan Varthaman) was shot down and its pilot captured. The pilot was released by Pakistan within 60 hours. The incident was unprecedented because: it was the first IAF strike inside Pakistan proper in 48 years; both sides exchanged airstrikes between nuclear-armed states; and de-escalation happened through diplomatic channels within days.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: The first Indian Air Force strike inside undisputed Pakistani territory since 1971 — both nuclear-armed states exchanged airstrikes before de-escalation through diplomacy within days
India's first use of nuclear weapons in a limited strike against a terrorist training camp, establishing a new precedent
A UN Security Council-authorised counter-terrorism strike that set an international precedent for pre-emptive action
A ground incursion by Indian Army special forces who captured Pakistani officers and exchanged them for Indian POWs

elimination_hints.hint: Balakot was a conventional airstrike — India has NFU and did not use nuclear weapons; the strike involved conventional air-dropped munitions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the unprecedented conventional cross-border airstrike with nuclear weapons use.

elimination_hints.hint: Balakot was a unilateral Indian action without UNSC authorisation — Pakistan condemned it as a violation of its sovereignty; there was no UN sanction for the strike.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume counter-terrorism strikes require UN authorisation when India acted unilaterally under its self-defence claim.

elimination_hints.hint: Balakot was an aerial strike, not a ground incursion; Pakistani officers were not captured — it was IAF pilot Abhinandan who was captured by Pakistan and subsequently released.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the Balakot aerial operation with ground special forces operations and reverse who captured whom.

question_text: People-to-people contacts between India and Pakistan — cultural exchanges, cricket, music, films — are often cited as a pathway to peace. What has been the actual impact of these exchanges on bilateral relations?
explanation: People-to-people contacts have had modest but real impacts: Indian and Pakistani cultures share deep affinities (film, music, food, language) that create mutual sympathy at the social level even as governments remain hostile. Indian Bollywood films and music have large Pakistani audiences; Pakistani cricketers and musicians have been popular in India. However, the actual impact on state-level relations has been limited: (1) governments have periodically banned cultural exchanges during tensions (Pakistan's cricket team was excluded from India's IPL from 2009, concerts were stopped after Uri 2016); (2) the social good will does not translate automatically into policy change; (3) state-level security establishments actively restrict exchanges to prevent normalisation they oppose; and (4) social media has also enabled communication but also hate speech and polarisation. The relationship between cultural exchange and political normalisation is non-linear.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Cultural exchanges have completely transformed the relationship — India and Pakistan now have fully normalised relations due to cricket and Bollywood
Cultural exchanges have had no impact whatsoever — South Asian populations feel no affinity and exchanges have never occurred

Cultural exchanges create genuine social-level good will but governments periodically restrict them, and social affinity has not translated into policy normalisation — the peace-through-people pathway faces structural state obstacles
Cultural exchanges have accelerated military conflict because they expose the two populations to each other's propaganda

elimination_hints.hint: India-Pakistan relations remain deeply hostile at the state level — cultural exchanges have not produced normalisation; trade is minimal, diplomats are withdrawn, and cross-border attacks continue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume social-level cultural affinity automatically translates into state-level political normalisation.

elimination_hints.hint: Significant cultural exchange has occurred — Pakistani artists (Atif Aslam, Rahat Fateh Ali Khan, Fawad Khan) were popular in India before government bans; Bollywood is widely watched in Pakistan.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume political hostility means no cultural exchange when popular culture has crossed borders despite state obstacles.

elimination_hints.hint: Cultural exchanges generally produce appreciation rather than conflict — the problem is that governments restrict them during tensions, not that the exchanges themselves create hostility.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reverse the direction of cultural exchange's impact — it generally creates empathy, and governments restrict it precisely because they fear the normalisation it might produce.

question_text:
Match the following key issues in India-Pakistan bilateral relations in Column A with their correct descriptions or status in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) Sir Creek | (P) High-altitude glacier occupied by India since 1984 — thousands of casualties from harsh conditions |
| (ii) Siachen Glacier | (Q) Narrow tidal estuary in Rann of Kutch — disputed maritime boundary affecting continental shelf claims |
| (iii) Wullar Barrage/Tulbul Project | (R) India's navigation project on Jhelum River in Kashmir — Pakistan objects as violation of Indus Waters Treaty |
| (iv) Kashmir (LoC) | (S) Core territorial dispute — India controls two-thirds, Pakistan one-third — both claim all |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: High-altitude glacier occupied by India since 1984 — thousands of casualties from harsh conditions
Narrow tidal estuary in Rann of Kutch — disputed maritime boundary affecting continental shelf claims
India's navigation project on Jhelum River in Kashmir — Pakistan objects as violation of Indus Waters Treaty
Core territorial dispute — India controls two-thirds, Pakistan one-third — both claim all

explanation: Sir Creek (i) is the disputed tidal estuary in the Rann of Kutch affecting maritime boundaries (Q). Siachen (ii) is the high-altitude glacier India has occupied since 1984 (P). Wullar Barrage/Tulbul (iii) is India's Jhelum navigation project Pakistan objects to under the Indus Waters Treaty (R). Kashmir LoC (iv) is the core territorial dispute with the line dividing Indian and Pakistani control (S).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S
i-R, ii-P, iii-Q, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: P describes Siachen (high-altitude glacier), not Sir Creek; Sir Creek is the tidal estuary in Rann of Kutch (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Sir Creek (maritime/tidal estuary) with Siachen (high-altitude glacier).

elimination_hints.hint: R describes Wullar Barrage (Jhelum navigation project), not Siachen; Siachen is the high-altitude glacier (P), not a dam or navigation project.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Siachen glacier dispute with the Wullar Barrage water infrastructure dispute.

elimination_hints.hint: R describes Wullar Barrage (Indus treaty objection), not Sir Creek; Sir Creek is about the maritime boundary in Rann of Kutch (Q), not water infrastructure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Sir Creek's maritime boundary dimension with the Wullar Barrage's water treaty dimension.

question_text:
Match the following key events or leaders in India-Pakistan post-Cold War relations in Column A with their correct descriptions in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) Benazir Bhutto | (P) Pakistani PM who signed Lahore Declaration with Vajpayee — removed by Musharraf coup 1999 |
| (ii) Nawaz Sharif | (Q) First female PM of Pakistan — twice elected, twice dismissed, assassinated 2007 |
| (iii) PM Modi's surprise Lahore visit | (R) December 2015 — unannounced stopover to attend Sharif's granddaughter's wedding, briefly reviving peace |
| (iv) Imran Khan | (S) Cricketer-turned-PM (2018-2022) — made overtures to India but relations remained frozen |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: Pakistani PM who signed Lahore Declaration with Vajpayee — removed by Musharraf coup 1999
First female PM of Pakistan — twice elected, twice dismissed, assassinated 2007
December 2015 — unannounced stopover to attend Sharif's granddaughter's wedding, briefly reviving peace
Cricketer-turned-PM (2018-2022) — made overtures to India but relations remained frozen

explanation: Benazir Bhutto (i) was Pakistan's first female PM, twice elected and dismissed, assassinated in 2007 (Q). Nawaz Sharif (ii) signed the Lahore Declaration and was ousted in Musharraf's 1999 coup (P). Modi's Lahore visit (iii) was the unannounced December 2015 stopover (R). Imran Khan (iv) was cricketer-PM 2018-2022 who made peace overtures but relations stayed frozen (S).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-S, iii-R, iv-P
i-R, ii-P, iii-Q, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: P describes Nawaz Sharif (signed Lahore Declaration, ousted 1999), not Benazir Bhutto; Benazir was Pakistan's first female PM who was assassinated (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Pakistan's two alternating PMs — Benazir Bhutto (PPP, first female PM) and Nawaz Sharif (PML-N, signed Lahore Declaration).

elimination_hints.hint: S describes Imran Khan (cricketer-PM 2018-22), not Nawaz Sharif; Nawaz Sharif signed the Lahore Declaration and was ousted by Musharraf (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Nawaz Sharif (Lahore Declaration, 1999 coup) with Imran Khan (later cricketer-PM).

elimination_hints.hint: R describes Modi's surprise Lahore visit (December 2015), not Benazir Bhutto; Benazir was assassinated in 2007 (Q), years before the 2015 Modi visit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the 2015 Modi Lahore visit with Benazir Bhutto's diplomatic role, perhaps because Benazir was also PM during some India-Pakistan dialogue periods.

question_text:
Match the following analytical frameworks for understanding India-Pakistan relations in Column A with their correct descriptions or proponents in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) 'Protracted social conflict' | (P) Bruce Riedel — Pakistan's army needs India as an existential enemy to justify its political dominance and massive defence budget |
| (ii) 'Enduring rivalry' | (Q) Edward Azar — deep-rooted communal identity conflict rooted in the trauma of partition that cannot be resolved through elite diplomacy |
| (iii) 'Army needs enemy' thesis | (R) India and Pakistan are locked in a long-term interstate rivalry with recurrent crises but short of total war |
| (iv) 'Strategic depth' concept | (S) Pakistan's traditional doctrine that Afghanistan must be strategically controlled to provide strategic depth against India in a two-front war |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: Bruce Riedel — Pakistan's army needs India as an existential enemy to justify its political dominance and massive defence budget
Edward Azar — deep-rooted communal identity conflict rooted in the trauma of partition that cannot be resolved through elite diplomacy
India and Pakistan are locked in a long-term interstate rivalry with recurrent crises but short of total war
Pakistan's traditional doctrine that Afghanistan must be strategically controlled to provide strategic depth against India in a two-front war

explanation: Protracted social conflict (i) is Edward Azar's concept for deep-rooted identity-based conflicts (Q). Enduring rivalry (ii) is the political science concept of long-term interstate rivalry with recurrent crises (R). Army needs enemy (iii) is Bruce Riedel's thesis about Pakistan military's institutional interest in India-hostility (P). Strategic depth (iv) is Pakistan's traditional Afghanistan doctrine (S).
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i-R, ii-Q, iii-P, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S
i-P, ii-R, iii-Q, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: R describes enduring rivalry (long-term interstate rivalry), not protracted social conflict; protracted social conflict is Azar's deep identity-based conflict concept (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse protracted social conflict (Azar's deep identity conflict theory) with enduring rivalry (interstate political science concept).

elimination_hints.hint: P describes army needs enemy thesis (Pakistan military institutional interest), not enduring rivalry; enduring rivalry is the interstate rivalry concept (R).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse enduring rivalry (systemic interstate theory) with the army needs enemy thesis (institutional interest explanation).

elimination_hints.hint: P describes army needs enemy thesis (Riedel's Pakistan military argument), not protracted social conflict; protracted social conflict is Azar's deep identity conflict concept (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse protracted social conflict (deep historical-psychological theory) with the army needs enemy thesis (institutional political economy argument).

assertion: The Kashmir dispute between India and Pakistan is not merely a territorial question but a fundamental clash of national identities — India's secular civic nationalism versus Pakistan's Islamic religious nationalism — making resolution structurally difficult.
reason: For India, accepting Pakistani sovereignty over Kashmir would validate the two-nation theory and undermine India's own identity as a secular, multi-religious democracy where Muslims are equal citizens; for Pakistan, accepting Indian sovereignty would raise the question of why Pakistan exists as a separate state at all.
question_text:
Assertion (A): The Kashmir dispute between India and Pakistan is not merely a territorial question but a fundamental clash of national identities — India's secular civic nationalism versus Pakistan's Islamic religious nationalism — making resolution structurally difficult.
Reason (R): For India, accepting Pakistani sovereignty over Kashmir would validate the two-nation theory and undermine India's own identity as a secular, multi-religious democracy where Muslims are equal citizens; for Pakistan, accepting Indian sovereignty would raise the question of why Pakistan exists as a separate state at all.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically sound and causally connected. R identifies the specific identity stakes for each country — India's secular national identity threatened by validating the two-nation theory; Pakistan's religious nationalist foundation undermined if Muslim-majority Kashmir remains with secular India — that directly explain A's claim about the structural difficulty of resolution. This is the most common scholarly explanation for why Kashmir cannot be resolved by purely territorial compromise.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R identifies the specific national identity stakes — India's secular identity vs two-nation theory validation, Pakistan's raison d'être challenged by Indian Kashmir — that directly explain A's structural difficulty claim.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's structural analysis and R's identity stakes as parallel rather than as explanation-explanandum.

elimination_hints.hint: R is analytically sound — India's secular nationalism would be fundamentally challenged if it conceded a Muslim-majority territory to Pakistan on religious grounds; Pakistan's Islamic nationalism foundation would be questioned if a Muslim-majority region chose to remain with secular India.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question whether the national identity implications in R are genuinely relevant to Kashmir's resolution difficulty.

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by the most influential Kashmir scholarship — both Sumit Ganguly and Praveen Swami have argued that Kashmir's difficulty lies in the identity clash, not just territorial stakes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reduce Kashmir to a simple territorial dispute, dismissing A's point about the deeper national identity implications for both sides.

assertion: The Indus Waters Treaty's survival through three India-Pakistan wars is evidence that technical and functional cooperation between adversaries is possible even without resolving core political disputes.
reason: The treaty created a Permanent Indus Commission with continuous technical dialogue, compartmentalised water management from the political conflict, and both sides recognised that disrupting water flows would cause immediate economic catastrophe in their own agricultural sectors — creating mutual interest in maintaining the functional arrangement.
question_text:
Assertion (A): The Indus Waters Treaty's survival through three India-Pakistan wars is evidence that technical and functional cooperation between adversaries is possible even without resolving core political disputes.
Reason (R): The treaty created a Permanent Indus Commission with continuous technical dialogue, compartmentalised water management from the political conflict, and both sides recognised that disrupting water flows would cause immediate economic catastrophe in their own agricultural sectors — creating mutual interest in maintaining the functional arrangement.
explanation: Both A and R are accurate and causally connected. R identifies the specific institutional mechanisms — Permanent Indus Commission, compartmentalisation, mutual economic interest — that explain how the treaty survived three wars without political resolution. R directly explains A's 'technical cooperation without political resolution' argument through the specific functional design features that insulated the treaty from political conflict.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R identifies the specific functional design features — Permanent Indus Commission, compartmentalisation, mutual economic interest — that directly explain why the treaty survived three wars as described in A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's analytical observation and R's institutional explanation as parallel rather than as evidence-conclusion.

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually documented — the Permanent Indus Commission has annual meetings, the treaty compartmentalises water from political issues, and both countries' agricultural sectors depend on Indus water.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question whether the Permanent Indus Commission's continuous functioning or the compartmentalisation design actually explain the treaty's resilience.

elimination_hints.hint: A is factually supported — the treaty has functioned continuously since 1960 through wars in 1965, 1971, and 1999, making it a documented example of technical cooperation transcending political hostility.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students dismiss A's claim about technical cooperation being possible despite political conflict when the Indus Treaty is the most cited South Asian example of exactly this phenomenon.

assertion: India's growing economic and military power relative to Pakistan has paradoxically made peace more difficult rather than easier, because as the power gap widens, Pakistan's military has become more dependent on asymmetric instruments — terrorism and nuclear deterrence — to maintain strategic parity.
reason: As India's economy became five to eight times larger than Pakistan's and its conventional military superiority grew, Pakistan responded by developing tactical nuclear weapons (Nasr missile) and supporting non-state actors as low-cost strategic tools — because it could no longer afford to compete symmetrically with India's conventional capabilities.
question_text:
Assertion (A): India's growing economic and military power relative to Pakistan has paradoxically made peace more difficult rather than easier, because as the power gap widens, Pakistan's military has become more dependent on asymmetric instruments — terrorism and nuclear deterrence — to maintain strategic parity.
Reason (R): As India's economy became five to eight times larger than Pakistan's and its conventional military superiority grew, Pakistan responded by developing tactical nuclear weapons (Nasr missile) and supporting non-state actors as low-cost strategic tools — because it could no longer afford to compete symmetrically with India's conventional capabilities.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically sound and causally connected. R identifies the specific strategic logic — widening economic/military gap driving Pakistan toward asymmetric tools — that directly explains A's 'power gap making peace harder' paradox. The strategic literature on Pakistan's nuclear and non-state actor strategies consistently cites asymmetric compensation for conventional inferiority as the driving logic.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R's logic — widening conventional gap driving Pakistan toward asymmetric tools — is precisely the causal mechanism for A's 'power gap makes peace harder' paradox.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's strategic paradox and R's strategic logic as parallel rather than as explanation-explanandum.

elimination_hints.hint: R is analytically documented — Pakistan developed Nasr tactical nuclear weapons specifically in response to India's Cold Start doctrine; and Pakistani army's support for non-state actors is a documented low-cost strategic tool.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question whether Pakistan's asymmetric strategy development was genuinely a response to widening conventional inferiority or simply ideological choice.

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by the standard strategic analysis of South Asia — Christopher Clary, Vipin Narang, and others have documented how growing power asymmetry in India's favour has driven Pakistan toward greater reliance on nuclear and non-state actor instruments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume greater Indian power would make Pakistan more accommodating rather than more desperate in its strategic calculations.

question_text:
Arrange the following events in the correct chronological sequence showing the evolution of India-Pakistan nuclear relations:

(i) India and Pakistan sign Lahore Declaration committing to nuclear restraint and CBMs
(ii) India conducts Pokhran-II nuclear tests — Pakistan responds with Chagai tests within days
(iii) Pakistan develops Nasr tactical nuclear missile — explicitly stated as response to India's Cold Start
(iv) Kargil War — world's first conventional conflict between two nuclear-armed states

items.text: India and Pakistan sign Lahore Declaration committing to nuclear restraint and CBMs
India conducts Pokhran-II nuclear tests — Pakistan responds with Chagai tests within days
Pakistan develops Nasr tactical nuclear missile — explicitly stated as response to India's Cold Start
Kargil War — world's first conventional conflict between two nuclear-armed states

explanation: Pokhran-II (India) was May 11-13, 1998; Pakistan's Chagai tests were May 28-30, 1998 (ii). Lahore Declaration was February 21, 1999 (i). Kargil War was May-July 1999 (iv). Pakistan's Nasr/Hatf-IX tactical nuclear missile was first tested in April 2011 (iii). This sequence shows nuclear testing → diplomatic stabilisation attempt → first nuclear-state conventional conflict → tactical nuclear escalation response.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: i → ii → iv → iii
ii → i → iv → iii
ii → iv → i → iii
i → iv → ii → iii

elimination_hints.hint: The nuclear tests (ii, May 1998) preceded the Lahore Declaration (i, February 1999) — the tests were the trigger for the subsequent nuclear CBM diplomacy, not a response to it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the diplomatic CBM effort before the nuclear tests that created the need for CBMs.

elimination_hints.hint: The Lahore Declaration (i, February 1999) preceded Kargil (iv, May-July 1999) — the peace declaration came before the war that violated its spirit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place Kargil before the Lahore Declaration when a key feature of the Kargil crisis is that the Declaration came just weeks before Pakistan's infiltration began.

elimination_hints.hint: The nuclear tests (ii, May 1998) preceded the Lahore Declaration (i, February 1999) — the entire sequence begins with the 1998 nuclear tests that changed the strategic landscape.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the diplomatic response (Lahore Declaration) before the nuclear test trigger that necessitated it.

question_text:
Arrange the following in the correct logical sequence showing how the 2019 Pulwama-Balakot crisis escalated and de-escalated:

(i) Pakistan releases IAF pilot Wing Commander Abhinandan — diplomatic de-escalation signal
(ii) JeM suicide bomber kills 40 CRPF personnel in Pulwama, Kashmir
(iii) Pakistan conducts retaliatory airstrikes — shoots down IAF MiG-21, captures pilot
(iv) Indian Air Force conducts Balakot airstrike on alleged JeM training camp in Pakistan

items.text: Pakistan releases IAF pilot Wing Commander Abhinandan — diplomatic de-escalation signal
JeM suicide bomber kills 40 CRPF personnel in Pulwama, Kashmir
Pakistan conducts retaliatory airstrikes — shoots down IAF MiG-21, captures pilot
Indian Air Force conducts Balakot airstrike on alleged JeM training camp in Pakistan

explanation: Pulwama attack was February 14, 2019 (ii). India's Balakot airstrike was February 26, 2019 (iv). Pakistan's retaliatory airstrikes were February 27, 2019, during which Abhinandan's aircraft was shot down and he was captured (iii). Pakistan released Abhinandan on March 1, 2019, as a peace gesture (i). This sequence shows the complete escalation-de-escalation cycle within just 15 days.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: ii → iv → iii → i
iv → ii → iii → i
ii → iii → iv → i
ii → iv → i → iii

elimination_hints.hint: Pulwama attack (ii, February 14) triggered India's Balakot strike (iv, February 26) — the attack must precede India's response.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place India's Balakot strike before the Pulwama attack that motivated it.

elimination_hints.hint: Pakistan's retaliatory airstrikes (iii, February 27) came after India's Balakot strike (iv, February 26) — Pakistan responded to India's strike, not before it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place Pakistan's retaliation before India's initial Balakot strike, reversing the escalation sequence.

elimination_hints.hint: Abhinandan's release (i, March 1) came after Pakistan captured him on February 27 (iii) — the release cannot precede the capture.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the pilot's release before Pakistan captured him during the February 27 aerial exchange.

question_text: India's revocation of Article 370 in August 2019 was universally welcomed internationally as a progressive step toward full constitutional integration of Jammu and Kashmir, and no major country formally criticised or objected to the move.
explanation: This statement is false. While India's domestic decision received support from its diaspora and some supporters of the BJP government's position, it generated significant international criticism: Pakistan immediately downgraded diplomatic relations, withdrew its ambassador, and sought to internationalise the issue through the UN Security Council, OIC (Organisation of Islamic Cooperation), and bilateral diplomatic channels. China expressed strong objections, particularly regarding the creation of the Union Territory of Ladakh which India carved out from J&K — China objected because Aksai Chin (which it administers) is part of the Ladakh territory India claimed. Turkey's President Erdogan and Malaysia's PM Mahathir raised the issue at the UN General Assembly. The UN Human Rights Office called for dialogue. The statement's claim of 'universal welcome' and 'no major country objecting' is historically false.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: True
False

question_text:
Study the following table showing the multiple dimensions of India-Pakistan bilateral relations across key indicators:

| Dimension | Status | Trajectory | Key Issue |
|-----------|--------|------------|----------|
| Political/Diplomatic | Severely restricted — ambassadors withdrawn | Deteriorating since 2019 | Article 370, Pakistan's internationalisation |
| Military | Hostile — four wars, Kargil 1999, Balakot 2019 | Tense but LoC ceasefire 2021 | Kashmir LoC, Siachen, Line of Actual Control |
| Nuclear | Both armed — India NFU, Pakistan first-use option | Dangerous — tactical nukes, low threshold | Stability-instability paradox |
| Trade | ~$2B (far below $37B potential) | Collapsed after 2019 MFN withdrawal | Restricted access, MFN revoked |
| Water | Indus Waters Treaty functioning since 1960 | Only surviving functional arrangement | India's dam projects challenged by Pakistan |
| People-to-people | Cricket, film, cultural affinity | Periodically suspended | Visa restrictions, government bans |
| Terrorism | Core obstacle — LeT, JeM attacks on India | No resolution — Hafiz Saeed free | India's terrorism-first precondition |
| Afghan dimension | Competing interests — India pro-Kabul, Pakistan pro-Taliban | Taliban return 2021 | Pakistan's strategic depth doctrine |

Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions is MOST accurately supported about India-Pakistan relations?
explanation: The table shows a deeply asymmetric relationship: almost every dimension is either deteriorating or frozen, with water (Indus Treaty) as the only functional cooperative arrangement. The contrast between the $37B potential and $2B actual trade reveals economic irrationality driven by political hostility. The table confirms the theoretical framework — India-Pakistan relations are trapped by structural obstacles (nuclear stability-instability paradox, terrorism as spoiler, identity-based Kashmir dispute, army institutional interests) that prevent normalisation despite clear economic and human cost. The only modest bright spot is the 2021 LoC ceasefire — but even this hasn't led to diplomatic progress.
subject: political-science
chapter: South Asia in the Post-Cold War Era

options.text: India-Pakistan relations are fundamentally normalising — the table shows mostly positive trajectories across all dimensions.
Water sharing (Indus Treaty) is the most hostile dimension — the table shows it is more deteriorated than military relations.
Trade is the primary obstacle — if trade were liberalised, all other dimensions would automatically normalise.

Relations are frozen or deteriorating across almost all dimensions, with water (Indus Treaty) as the only functional cooperative arrangement — structural obstacles across nuclear, terrorism, and Kashmir dimensions prevent comprehensive normalisation.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows 'Deteriorating since 2019' for political/diplomatic, 'Tense' for military, 'Collapsed after 2019' for trade — the trajectories are overwhelmingly negative, directly contradicting 'fundamentally normalising'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students misread the table's trajectory column which shows mostly negative or stagnant trends.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows Indus Treaty as 'Only surviving functional arrangement' with the status of 'functioning since 1960' — this is the most positive dimension, not the most hostile.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students misread the table and identify the only cooperative dimension (Indus Treaty) as hostile.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows trade is one of eight dimensions, and the nuclear, terrorism, and Kashmir dimensions have their own independent structural obstacles that would not automatically resolve with trade liberalisation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students apply liberal trade-leads-to-peace theory without recognising that the specific structural obstacles the table identifies (nuclear, terrorism, Kashmir) cannot be overcome through trade alone.
